lifecycle_current: The virtual data warehouse concept evolved into data virtualization and, more recently, data fabric architectures. The specific Intersolv product was discontinued after Merant/Micro Focus restructuring.
notes: description=A decision-support environment using metadata to provide unified access to distributed data sources without physical data consolidation, introduced October 2, 1995.; lifecycle_note=The virtual data warehouse concept evolved into data virtualization and, more recently, data fabric architectures. The specific Intersolv product was discontinued after Merant/Micro Focus restructuring.; successor_technology=data-virtualization / data-fabric

lifecycle_current: Specific SmartData branding discontinued post-merger; metadata management capabilities absorbed into subsequent DataDirect products.
notes: description=Intelligent metadata layer implemented as an ODBC driver that shields users from SQL complexity and creates virtual databases (SmartSets) over physical data sources.; lifecycle_note=Specific SmartData branding discontinued post-merger; metadata management capabilities absorbed into subsequent DataDirect products.; successor_technology=DataDirect ODBC drivers

lifecycle_current: Explorer desktop product discontinued following Merant restructuring; capabilities partially absorbed into successor BI tooling.
notes: description=Desktop query, analysis, and reporting tool providing Query Builder, Chart Builder, Report Builder, Form Builder, and Job Scheduler components.; lifecycle_note=Explorer desktop product discontinued following Merant restructuring; capabilities partially absorbed into successor BI tooling.; successor_technology=ODBC-compliant BI tools

lifecycle_current: ODBC driver product line continues under Progress DataDirect brand, supporting modern databases and cloud sources.
notes: description=Set of ODBC drivers supporting more than 35 databases on eight operating systems, connecting any ODBC-compliant application to personal, relational, or legacy databases.; lifecycle_note=ODBC driver product line continues under Progress DataDirect brand, supporting modern databases and cloud sources.; successor_technology=Progress DataDirect Drivers

lifecycle_current: SequeLink product was eventually discontinued after Intersolv/Merant transitions; server-side connectivity approach supplanted by modern middleware.
notes: description=Server-based database-independent middleware for client-server connectivity, enabling heterogeneous joins, cursor management, and legacy integration without performance-degrading gateways.; lifecycle_note=SequeLink product was eventually discontinued after Intersolv/Merant transitions; server-side connectivity approach supplanted by modern middleware.; successor_technology=JDBC/ODBC middleware; modern data integration platforms

lifecycle_current: ODBC remains an active and widely supported standard for database connectivity, supplemented by JDBC and newer APIs.
notes: description=Standard API for accessing database management systems, allowing applications to connect to any ODBC-compliant data source regardless of database vendor.; lifecycle_note=ODBC remains an active and widely supported standard for database connectivity, supplemented by JDBC and newer APIs.; successor_technology=JDBC; cloud data APIs; ODBC 4.0

lifecycle_current: SQL remains the dominant query language for relational databases; standardized through ANSI/ISO; extended with window functions, JSON support, and other features.
notes: description=Standard language for relational database queries; the Virtual Data Warehouse was designed to shield business users from writing SQL directly.; lifecycle_note=SQL remains the dominant query language for relational databases; standardized through ANSI/ISO; extended with window functions, JSON support, and other features.; successor_technology=SQL:2023 standard

lifecycle_current: RDBMSs remain foundational; augmented by columnar stores (Redshift, BigQuery, Snowflake) optimized for analytical workloads.
notes: description=Class of database systems based on relational model; referenced as the primary foundation for physical data warehouses.; lifecycle_note=RDBMSs remain foundational; augmented by columnar stores (Redshift, BigQuery, Snowflake) optimized for analytical workloads.; successor_technology=Columnar / analytical RDBMS; cloud data warehouses

lifecycle_current: Dedicated MDDBs largely replaced by columnar databases with OLAP extensions; concept persists in cloud OLAP engines.
notes: description=Proprietary database optimized for OLAP; referenced as alternative to RDBMS for data warehousing (e.g., Cognos Powerplay).; lifecycle_note=Dedicated MDDBs largely replaced by columnar databases with OLAP extensions; concept persists in cloud OLAP engines.; successor_technology=OLAP engines; columnar databases

lifecycle_current: Metadata management has grown in importance; modern data catalogs (Apache Atlas, Alation, Collibra) and data governance platforms are direct successors.
notes: description=Practice of cataloging and managing data about data; central to the Virtual Data Warehouse's SmartData semantic layer approach.; lifecycle_note=Metadata management has grown in importance; modern data catalogs (Apache Atlas, Alation, Collibra) and data governance platforms are direct successors.; successor_technology=Data catalogs; data governance platforms

lifecycle_current: OLE 2.0 succeeded by COM, then .NET, then modern web embedding standards. Still supported in Windows but no longer a primary integration approach.
notes: description=Object Linking and Embedding standard for embedding objects (e.g., Word documents) within applications; used by Explorer Report Builder.; lifecycle_note=OLE 2.0 succeeded by COM, then .NET, then modern web embedding standards. Still supported in Windows but no longer a primary integration approach.; successor_technology=COM; .NET; modern web embedding

lifecycle_current: IBM Db2 remains an active product; modernized with cloud and containerized deployments.
notes: description=IBM relational database management system; referenced as a snapshot source in the Intelligent Snapshot feature.; lifecycle_note=IBM Db2 remains an active product; modernized with cloud and containerized deployments.; successor_technology=IBM Db2 (cloud editions)

lifecycle_current: Cognos Impromptu discontinued after IBM acquisition; capabilities merged into IBM Cognos BI suite.
notes: description=Query and reporting tool from Cognos Corp., a direct competitor to DataDirect Explorer; noted for stronger enterprise metadata dissemination features.; lifecycle_note=Cognos Impromptu discontinued after IBM acquisition; capabilities merged into IBM Cognos BI suite.; successor_technology=IBM Cognos Analytics

lifecycle_current: BusinessObjects survived acquisition by SAP; continues as SAP BusinessObjects BI platform.
notes: description=BI platform from Business Objects; a direct competitor to the Virtual Data Warehouse; noted for strong semantic layer but tool-specific metadata.; lifecycle_note=BusinessObjects survived acquisition by SAP; continues as SAP BusinessObjects BI platform.; successor_technology=SAP BusinessObjects BI

lifecycle_current: Mercury was released but later discontinued; concept of microcubes evolved into local OLAP/in-memory analytics.
notes: description=Decision-support system combining intelligent metadata with desktop microcubes for a virtual multidimensional environment; announced for early 1996 release.; lifecycle_note=Mercury was released but later discontinued; concept of microcubes evolved into local OLAP/in-memory analytics.; successor_technology=In-memory analytics; Power BI; Tableau
